key_insight: The paper turns multimaterial 3D printing design into an end-to-end optimization problem by learning closed-form, composition-aware constitutive laws and coupling them directly to topology optimization.
engineering_relevance: This enables simultaneous optimization of shape and material distribution for soft robotic structures, reducing reliance on slow trial-and-error prototyping.
method: Train sparsified physics-augmented neural networks on experimental data to recover interpretable hyperelastic laws.
Use symbolic differentiation and an adjoint method in FEniCSx to couple the constitutive model with finite-element topology optimization.
Apply the framework to soft robotic grippers for continuous composition design and topology/material co-optimization under stretch constraints.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports demonstration on soft robotic gripper applications, including continuous composition optimization and concurrent topology/material optimization under non-failure stretch limits.
watch_item: The approach depends on extracting accurate closed-form constitutive laws from data, so model fidelity and identifiability may be challenging for more complex materials or sparse experiments.
paper_title: Towards end-to-end optimization in multimaterial 3D printing
